frameworks.name: higher-order theory of subjective experience
frameworks.one_line: subjective fear depends on prefrontal implicit metacognition over visual representations

frameworks.name: multi-voxel pattern analysis of ventral visual stream
frameworks.one_line: ventral stream encodes fear-correlated visual features without encoding fear experience itself

frameworks.name: multivariate information synchronization (functional connectivity)
frameworks.one_line: PFC-visual cortex coupling indexes whether participants subjectively experience fear

frameworks.name: hardwired threat-detection account
frameworks.one_line: early perception alone does not generate subjective fear

core_question: Does the ventral visual stream itself generate the subjective experience of fear, or does subjective fear instead require interaction between visual cortex and prefrontal cortex?
key_assumptions: subjective fear is dissociable from threat-feature encoding
idiosyncratic per-participant fear profiles can be used to label stimuli
multi-voxel patterns reflect feature-level visual representation
cross-region multivariate synchronization indexes information sharing
manipulations: fear vs no-fear participant grouping by idiosyncratic fear profile
animal images that did vs did not trigger subjective fear per individual
region-of-interest: ventral visual stream vs prefrontal cortex
key_findings: ventral visual stream patterns represent affectively neutral visual features statistically tied to fear ratings, not the fear experience itself
multivariate information synchronization between ventral visual and prefrontal regions distinguishes fearful from non-fearful participants
interpretations: subjective fear arises when visual information engages implicit metacognitive mechanisms in prefrontal cortex
early visual cortex alone is insufficient for the subjective experience of fear despite carrying threat-correlated features
